coredata.dc:title: Exploring forward scattering mechanisms in TiO2 with carbon quantum dots: Insights into photovoltaic applications
coredata.dc:creator: Samir, Mona
Ismail, Zahraa
Agour, Ahmed M.
Nageh, Hassan
Abdellatif, Sameh O.
coredata.dc:description: The study investigates the forward scattering mechanisms in TiO2 with CQDs and their potential implications for photovoltaic applications. Thin films fabricated on microscopic glass were experimentally characterized using UV–Vis-IR spectrometer and SEM measurements. The experimental data was used to determine the effective scattering particle radius through Mie scattering modeling. At the same time, a convolutional neural network-based algorithm and image post-processing were employed to study the thin film porosity. Machine learning models were then utilized to aggregate the data and identify the optimum CQD concentration for maximum forward scattering. The extinction spectra were found to be significantly dependent on the concentration of CQDs in the composite, with forward scattering dominating for wavelengths above 400 nm. The study also utilized a random-forest machine learning algorithm to predict the impact of varying CQD concentrations, providing insights beyond the dataset's limit of up to 15 %. Additionally, numerical simulations using finite difference time domain analysis and MEEP were employed to provide extinction spectra for different CQD concentrations, ultimately identifying an optimum CQD concentration of 6.9 % for maximum extinction performance. The study also analyzed the tradeoff between the effective scattering particle radius and porosity against CQDs concentration, shedding light on the impact of these parameters on forward scattering.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Forward scattering
UV–Vis–NIR spectrometer
SEM measurements
Mie scattering model
Convolutional neural network
Machine learning
Porosity
Effective scattering particle radius

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the support and contribution of The Centre of Emerging Learning Technologies (CELT) , FabLab , and The Nanotechnology Research Centre (NTRC) at the British University in Egypt . Special thanks to Ms. Mona Samir, research assistant at NTRC, for her support in preparing NiO suspension.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This paper investigates the overall experimental, numerical, and ML-based optimization process for TiO2 thin films with CQDs under various concentrations.
(a) Controlled screen-printing setup for Depositing mesoporous TiO2 sample on microscopic glass, and (b) mesoporous TiO2 sample on microscopic glass.
Transmission light measurements with variable sample positions to investigate the forward scattering in the samples under test.
A demonstrative schematic for the optical model scripted on MEEP to simulate forward scattering due to a thin film of TiO2 with variation in CQDS concentration from 0 % to 15 %.
SEM measurements for bare TiO2 thin film (a), and (c), and TiO2 with 3 % CQD concentration (b), and (d).
SEM measurements for (a) bare TiO2 thin film, (b) TiO2 with 1 % CQD concentration, and (c), TiO2 with 3 % CQD concentration.

XRD measurement for bare TiO2. In the XRD pattern of bare TiO2, distinct peaks were observed at specific 2θ values, namely 25.2°, 37.6°, 47.9°, 53.7°, 54.8°, 62.6°, and 74.8°. These peaks were assigned to the crystallographic planes (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), and (215) of anatase TiO2, respectively.
EDX-mapping measurements for (a) bare TiO2 thin film, (b) TiO2 with 3 % CQD concentration, and (c), TiO2 with 9 % CQD concentration.

UV–Vis–NIR spectrometer measurements, using the measurement procedure in Fig. 3, for (a) bare TiO2 thin film, (b) TiO2 with 1 % CQD concentration, (c), TiO2 with 3 % CQD concentration, (d) (c), TiO2 with 9 % CQD concentration, and (e) (c), TiO2 with 12 % CQD concentration.
(a) UV–Vis–NIR spectrometer transmission measurements against simulated data or 0 %, 1 %, 3 %, 9 %, and 12 % CQD concentration, and (b) corresponding extinction spectra.

Random-0forest ML outputs: (a) The extinction variation against both wavelength and CQD concentration from 0 % to 15 %, (b) Extinction variation against the CQD concentration, at 550 nm central wavelength, (c) Extinction spectrum at CQD concentration of 6.9 %, (d)the trade-off between effective radius and porosity while increasing the CQD concentration.

thin films parameters associated with the five sets of fabricated samples extracted from SEM and UV–Vis-IR measurements. Hint: the effective scattering particle radius is a fitted parameter based on the Mie scattering model, with fitting RMSE indicated.

row.entry: Scattering particle radius as extracted from SEM (nm)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Our proposed scattering medium of TiO2, with CQDs, against previous attempts in the literature.

row.entry: Variable thickness from 77 nm to 305 nm with minimum transmission dip of 45 %.
TiO2 thin films integrated with Au plasmonic nanoparticles
TiO2 thin films integrated with CQDs with varying concentration from 0 % to 12 %
An extinction forward scattering extinction value of 5.3 (230 % of the bare value) with a CQD concentration of 6.9 %

head.ce:title: Exploring forward scattering mechanisms in TiO2 with carbon quantum dots: Insights into photovoltaic applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study investigates the forward scattering mechanisms in TiO2 with CQDs and their potential implications for photovoltaic applications. Thin films fabricated on microscopic glass were experimentally characterized using UV–Vis-IR spectrometer and SEM measurements. The experimental data was used to determine the effective scattering particle radius through Mie scattering modeling. At the same time, a convolutional neural network-based algorithm and image post-processing were employed to study the thin film porosity. Machine learning models were then utilized to aggregate the data and identify the optimum CQD concentration for maximum forward scattering. The extinction spectra were found to be significantly dependent on the concentration of CQDs in the composite, with forward scattering dominating for wavelengths above 400 nm. The study also utilized a random-forest machine learning algorithm to predict the impact of varying CQD concentrations, providing insights beyond the dataset's limit of up to 15 %. Additionally, numerical simulations using finite difference time domain analysis and MEEP were employed to provide extinction spectra for different CQD concentrations, ultimately identifying an optimum CQD concentration of 6.9 % for maximum extinction performance. The study also analyzed the tradeoff between the effective scattering particle radius and porosity against CQDs concentration, shedding light on the impact of these parameters on forward scattering.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Porous titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been deeply introduced in the literature as a high-band gap, n-type, semiconductor material [1] that can be used in a wide range of optoelectronic properties [2–5]. Porous TiO2, characterized by its high surface area and interconnected pore structure, has garnered significant interest in various fields due to its exceptional properties [1]. These porous features enable a large number of active sites for catalysis and adsorption, making it a promising material for applications such as environmental remediation [6], energy storage [7], and photovoltaics [2,4,8]. The high surface area and porosity of TiO2 facilitate enhanced mass transport and light harvesting, making it an ideal candidate for use in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) [2,4,8], water splitting [9] and pollutant degradation [10]. Its tunable pore size and structure further contribute to its versatility, allowing for tailored applications in diverse technological and environmental contexts.

Moreover, TiO2 is addressed in the literature as a potential electron transport layer (ETL) in perovskite solar cells (PSC) [11,12]. TiO2 has emerged as a critical component in perovskite solar cells, where it serves as an electron transport layer due to its excellent electron mobility, high transparency, and suitable energy levels [13,14]. As an ETL, TiO2 facilitates the extraction and transport of electrons from the perovskite layer to the external circuit, thereby contributing to efficient electricity generation. Its wide bandgap and favorable conduction band alignment enable efficient electron injection and collection. At the same time, its porous nature allows for increased surface area and improved perovskite infiltration, leading to enhanced charge extraction and reduced recombination losses [5,13,14]. Using TiO2 as an ETL in perovskite solar cells has thus played a pivotal role in advancing the performance and stability of these promising photovoltaic devices.

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) use in photovoltaic applications has gained significant attention due to their unique optical and electronic properties [15,16]. CQDs represent a novel category of zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures characterized by a sp2-conjugated structure surrounded by oxygen-containing hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [17]. Initially discovered in 2004 during the purification of single-wall nanotubes, CQDs are defined as discrete quasi-spherical particles smaller than 10 nm [17]. Research has indicated that CQDs act as electron mediators, effectively preventing the recombination of photoexcited charge pairs [18]. Their exceptional physicochemical properties, such as low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, chemical inertness, biocompatibility, high photostability, and tunable photoluminescence, have positioned CQDs as a promising area of study in nanoscience applications, particularly in biosensing and cellular imaging [18]. Despite these advantages, challenges related to poor recyclability and limited photosensitization activity may hinder the widespread application of CQDs [19]. To address these limitations, the incorporation of nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus through heterogeneous doping has emerged as a promising strategy to enhance the electrical and optical characteristics of CQDs [17,19]. Among these dopants, nitrogen is particularly favored due to its high quantum yield and its ability to customize the electron transport characteristics of CQDs [17,19]. Several synthesis techniques, including laser ablation, electrochemical carbonization, hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment, and microwave irradiation, have proven effective in producing CQDs from both synthetic and natural materials [20,21]. These methods offer simple and cost-effective approaches using organic molecules, natural products, and readily accessible bioresources [22,23]. Moreover, CQDs are finding applications in diverse fields such as environmental remediation, biosensing, chemical sensing, bioimaging, medical diagnosis, photocatalysis, and photovoltaic devices, reflecting their potential to contribute to various technological advancements [19,23,24].

In principle, mesoporous TiO2 is a high-volume scattering medium [1]. In both DSSCs [25] and PSCs [11], TiO2 acts as a front layer concerning the active absorber. In this regard, layer transparency is essential to ensure photon transmission to the active medium [3]. Enhanced performance of solar cells can be achieved by accelerating the forward scattering mechanism. Deviating light beams with a high incident angle elongate the optical path length inside the active medium, reflecting the probability of photon capturing [26,27]. One of the most promising applications of CQDs is enhancing the performance of TiO2-based solar cells, which are introduced as meso-structured solar cells [1,4]. Embedding CQDs in the porous structure of the TiO2 highly impacted the scattering performance of such layer; this was significantly studied in quantum dot solar cells as in Ref. [28]. However, the impact of varying the CQD concentration on the underlying scattering mechanisms that govern the interaction between CQDs and TiO2 is poorly understood.

This study investigates the forward scattering mechanisms in TiO2 with CQDs and their potential implications for photovoltaic applications. Experimentally fabricated thin films on microscopic glass are characterized using UV–Vis-IR spectrometer and SEM measurements. Experimental data is then fitted to adopt the effective scattering particle radius using the Mie scattering model. Alternatively, the porosity of the thin films is studied with the aid of a convolutional neural network (CNN) based algorithm and image post-processing. Extracted data is seeded to the machine learning model for data aggregation, seeking the optimum CQD concentration for maximum forward scattering. The scattering mechanisms are investigated experimentally, using the spectrometer extinction measurements, while finite difference time domain analysis was enrolled for numerical simulation. The root-mean-square error (RSME) was calculated to validate the numerical approach concerning the experimental measurements using five different CQD concentrations. Fig. 1 illustrates the complete process addressed in this paper.

In the current study, we investigated the integration of N-doped carbon quantum dots in the mesoporous TiO2 suspension. The bare TiO2 suspension is prepared as reported in our previous work in Refs. [1–3,8]. Triton X is used to prepare a TiO2 suspension. Firstly, the TiO2 powder (commercially purchased from CDH, Product code: 030446, white/almost white powder) is dispersed in ethanol as a solvent. We then add a small amount of Triton X surfactant to the mixture while stirring continuously to ensure proper dispersion. The Triton X helps to stabilize the TiO2 particles in the suspension, preventing agglomeration and ensuring a uniform dispersion [1,4].

A straightforward and practical approach has been developed to synthesize N-doped carbon quantum dots using a hydrothermal method that eliminates the need for solvents [29]. In this process, 6 g of urea and 3 g of citric acid are utilized as nitrogen and carbon sources, respectively. These precursors are combined in 40 ml of deionized water and gently stirred for 1 h. The resulting mixture is then placed in a stainless-steel Teflon container and subjected to thermal treatment at 160 °C for 3 h, yielding a dark greenish colloidal solution of N-doped CQDs. Subsequently, the suspension is centrifuged at 12,000 rpm to eliminate large particles, and the remaining solution is meticulously washed with ethanol five times to remove any unreacted precursors. This method presents a simple and efficient means of producing N-doped carbon quantum dots aqueous solution, thus offering an environmentally friendly and cost-effective approach [29]. The current study proposes the enrollment of the N-doped CQDs in the mesoporous paste with variable concentrations ranging from 0 % to 12 %.

The prepared bare suspension and TiO2 suspension mixed with CQDs are all deposited using our automatically controlled screen-printing setup, previously demonstrated in Refs. [3,11]. A driving motor controls a glass rod, where the voltage supplied can control the printing speed. The deposition process, as well as the output samples, are shown in Fig. 2 a and b, respectively. For characterization, the physicochemical properties of the prepared samples were analyzed using SEM (Quattro s-Thermo Scientific). At the same time, optical spectra are measured using a UV–Vis-IR spectrometer (300 nm–3500 nm wavelength). As discussed earlier, the main target of this study is to explore the forward scattering mechanism in the TiO2 thin films with variable concentrations of CQDs. Therefore, we adopted the spectrometer to investigate such capabilities. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 , the sample is measured at different positions concerning the light source, starting with an extreme light position, 3 cm to the source, up to the extreme left position, and 12 cm to the source. The transmission spectra at these positions should record nearly the same values for a dominating absorption or backward scattering. However, in a forward scattering dominating sample, the closer to the detector, the higher the transmission. As the UV-VIS-IR setup is arranged to measure the transmission beam intensity (
T
), the extinction (
E
) can be calculated as [1]:
(1)
E = − 10
log
( T )

Herein, the extinction spectrum indicates the combination of various absorption and scattering components associated with the sample under investigation. Considering TiO2 is a comprehensive band gap semiconductor material with a UV absorption profile up to 400 nm, the extinction behavior with wavelengths higher than 400 nm is mainly attributed to scattering.

Following the general methodology presented in Fig. 1, two stages of optical modeling are addressed in the current study. Considering the proposed structure as a volume scattering medium with effective particle size in the wavelength range, we adopted the Mie scattering model to describe the forward scattering associated with the fabricated thin films. The MATLAB code in Refs. [1,30,31] fits the extinction spectrum concerning the experimentally measured extinction while considering the effective scattering particle radius, the prefactor, and the refractive index as the inputs. The prefactor is a mathematical parameter that describes the combination of some physical properties, including fractional volume porosity, the thin film thickness, and the effective aperture area of the detector. The effective particle radius and film porosity are calculated using the previously described SEM post-processing algorithm in Ref. [32]. Alternatively, the effective refractive index for the bare TiO2 was captured from our previous study in Ref. [1]. In contrast, the refractive index for other structures with CQDs was calculated based on the
T − λ
spectra with varying angles of incidence, as described in Ref. [1]. It is worth highlighting that the detector aperture area and thin film thickness are kept constant across all experiments conducted in this study.

On the other hand, while considering limited fabricated samples at CQDs concentrations of 0 % (bare sample), 1 %, 3 %, 9 %, and 12 %, we utilized MEEP to study scattering mechanisms due to CQDs concentration varying from 0 % to 15 %. MEEP is a powerful open-source software package for simulating electromagnetic systems, including light propagation. It is beneficial for simulating the behavior of optical systems, including light scattering by various materials. One application of MEEP is in the simulation of forward scattering due to thin films, see Fig. 4 . By modeling the optical properties of the thin film and the surrounding medium, MEEP can predict the scattering behavior of light in various geometries. In the current study, we adopt the definition of scattered beams as those with deviation greater than or equal to 45°, following our work in Ref. [25]. A Gaussian source with a central wavelength at 550 nm is simulated to inject the sample optically. At the same time, probe detectors are added on the counter side to observe the forward scattering, cf—Fig. 4. The seed data associated with the thin films under various CQDS concentrations (from 0 % to 15 %) are collected as an output of a random forest machine learning algorithm described in the next section, where experimental data at 0 %, 1 %, 3 %, 9 %, and 12 % are used for validating the simulation results. A complete validation process is presented in the results and discussion section.

As stated earlier, five different experiments have been conducted in this study to investigate the impact of varying the CQDs concentration in porous TiO2 thin films on the forward scattering mechanisms. Referring to the introduced application of the porous structure as a front electron transport layer in solar cell applications, maximizing the forward scattering can boost a cell's overall power conversion efficiency. Accordingly, reaching the maximum CQDs concentration at which the forward scattering is needed. Considering limited experimental attempts, we utilized random forest [3,33–35] as a robust machine learning algorithm to enlarge the dataset internally, i.e., from 0 % to 12 %, or even externally, above 12 %. The random forest machine learning algorithm is known for its capabilities to effectively handle data sets, including scenarios where the dataset is limited in size [33–35]. Through the ensemble learning technique, random forest \generates multiple decision trees and aggregates their predictions, which can help in enlarging the dataset virtually. This approach can provide more robust and accurate predictions by capturing a more comprehensive range of data patterns and relationships, effectively expanding the model's capabilities beyond the confines of the original dataset.

Additionally, the random forest algorithm's ability to handle high-dimensional data and non-linear relationships further enhances its predictive capabilities, allowing it to generalize well and make accurate predictions even for data points outside the given dataset. Herein, we dataset the CQDs concentration against the effective radius of the scattering particles and the prefactor, as one input two outputs dataset. The algorithm is customized to provide a full macroscopic view of the impact of the CQDs on both outputs. In addition, the prediction capabilities of the model are integrated to extend the limit up to 15 %, 3 % higher than the experimental dataset.

Alternatively, another AI-post-processing model is used to classify and analyze SEM measurements. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) [36,37] have emerged as a powerful tool for image analysis and feature extraction. They are particularly well-suited for post-processing SEM measurements to predict features related to the concentration of CQDs in TiO2 thin films, cf—Fig. 1. By leveraging the hierarchical pattern recognition capabilities of CNNs, SEM images of the thin films are effectively analyzed to extract intricate features related to the distribution and concentration of CQDs within the TiO2 matrix [36,37]. The CNN model learns and discerns complex spatial patterns and structures, enabling it to identify and predict CQD-related features such as aggregation, dispersion, and porosity within the thin films. This predictive capability provides valuable insights for optimizing the fabrication process and properties of CQD-doped TiO2 thin films for various applications, including photovoltaics, sensors, and optoelectronic devices. In addition, these extracted features are treated as inputs for the Mie scattering model, seeking optimum fitting against the experimental extinction spectra.

In this section, we illustrated all experimental and numerical results extracted from this study. Experimentally, five samples were prepared as suspensions and deposited on a glass substrate, as shown in Fig. 2. The five sets are dominated by mesoporous TiO2, with various CQD concentrations, including 0 %, 1 %, 3 %, 9 %, and 12 %. To study the forward scattering mechanisms in the prepared thin films, the two experimental measurement pillars are the SEM and UV–Vis-IR spectrometer measurements. SEM measurements are conducted across the five different sets, where measurement samples are demonstrated in Figs. 5 and 6
. A microscopic comparison showing the effect of the CQDs concentration of 3 % concerning the bare sample is presented in Fig. 5. Alternatively, macroscopic 50 μm scale SEM images for the 0 %, 1 %, and 3 % are shown in Fig. 6. As described earlier in section 3, SEM measurements are mainly analogized to feed the numerical optical model with a set of parameters, including the scattering particle radius and the porosity. Applying our devolved algorithm in Ref. [32], the extracted SEM images are post-processed to estimate the highlighted feature parameters, as listed in Table 1 . Moreover, CNN is enrolled to classify the image features based on the CQDs concentration, making it possible to identify the variation in the physiochemical, morphological, and optical properties variation under different CQDs concentrations. The generated dataset was established based on the available five categories, where the porosity can be linked with the quantum dot concentration. This is discussed later in this section.

Crystalline TiO2, such as the anatase, display sharp and distinct diffraction peaks in their XRD patterns due to their well-defined crystal structures. These diffraction peaks are associated with the periodic arrangement of atoms in the crystal lattice, leading to constructive interference of X-rays. When analyzing XRD data for TiO2, as in Fig. 7 , the XRD pattern of bare TiO2, distinct peaks were observed at specific 2θ values, namely 25.2°, 37.6°, 47.9°, 53.7°, 54.8°, 62.6°, and 74.8°. These peaks were assigned to the crystallographic planes (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), and (215) of anatase TiO2, respectively [38].

Recognizing the insertion of carbon quantum dots with low concentrations, specifically below 15 %, in TiO2 using X-ray diffraction can be challenging due to several factors. Initially, at low concentrations, the signal from the CQDs may be overshadowed by the dominant diffraction peaks of TiO2. This can make it difficult to distinguish the contribution of the CQDs to the overall XRD pattern, especially if the CQDs do not exhibit strong diffraction features, due to the amorphous structure. Carbon quantum dots, especially those with low concentrations, may exhibit an amorphous or disordered structure, leading to a broad and weak scattering pattern in the XRD data. This can further complicate the differentiation of CQD-specific peaks from the background noise and the diffraction peaks of TiO2.

To overcome these challenges and improve the detection and identification of CQDs at low concentrations in TiO2 using XRD, complementary characterization technique such as EDX-Mapping is employed, cf. Fig. 8 . In the context of observing the mapping of carbon quantum dots with TiO2 in three samples—bare TiO2 (Fig. 8-a), TiO2 with 3 % CQDs (Fig. 8-b), and TiO2 with 9 % CQDs (Fig. 8-c)—the analysis of the Ti, O, and C content provides valuable insights into the compositional variations. In the bare TiO2 sample, the dominant elements are titanium (Ti) and oxygen (O), consistent with the composition of TiO2. The presence of Ti and O is expected to be prominent, reflecting the composition of the TiO2 material. In the sample containing TiO2 with 3 % CQDs, an observable increase in the carbon (C) content due to the addition of CQDs is detected. The C content is higher compared to the bare TiO2 sample, reflecting the incorporation of CQDs into the TiO2 matrix. As the CQD concentration increases to 9 % in the third sample, the C content would further rise, indicating a higher proportion of carbon within the composite material. Simultaneously, the Ti and O content remains consistent with the TiO2 matrix, although potential subtle variations in Ti and O content arises due to interactions between the CQDs and TiO2.

The effective refractive index for these samples should be calculated to seed the extracted parameters from the SEM to the Mie scattering model. In this regard, our previous work in Ref. [1] presented a simple technique to calculate the effective refractive index of the mesoporous TiO2, reaching a value of 1.623 ± 0.002. We utilized the Fresnel formula for calculating the effective refractive index. The transmitted power,
T
S R L
, where SRL stands for support reflection loss, is described as [1]:
(2)
T
S R L
= 1 −
R
B K 7
−
R
m p / B K 7
(3)
R
B K 7
=
(
n
B K 7
− 1
n
B K 7
+ 1
)
2
(4)
R
m p / B K 7
=
(
n
m p − C Q D
−
n
B K 7
n
m p − C Q D
+
n
B K 7
)
2
where
R
B K 7
is the reflectance between air and the glass substrate (BK7),
R
m p / B K 7
stands for the reflectance between the BK7 and the mesoporous layer, and
n
m p − C Q D
is the effective refractive index of the sample under test. The value of
R
B K 7
is equal to 0.04 [39]. Herein, we adopt the highlighted technique to estimate the effective refractive index for the new campsite with various CQDs concentrations, see Table 1.

Optically, we initially explore the forward scattering capabilities for the five sets of fabricated samples using the proposed measurement procedure described in section 2 and demonstrated in Fig. 3. Obeying the output spectra in Fig. 9 , all recorded data verifies the domination of the forward scattering as the transmission intensity increases by moving the samples closer to the detector. However, it can still be seen that the scattering intensities vary from one set to another based on the CQDs. Secondly, we feed the Mie scattering model [1,30,31] with the data listed in Table 1 while considering the effective scattering particle radius as the leading free parameter towards minimizing the root-mean-square error (RSME) agonist the experimentally measured extinction spectrum, see Fig. 10 . Per set of samples, the effective scattering particle radius is fitted with minimum RSME as indicated in Table 1. Fig. 10-a shows the experimental transmission spectra agonist and the corresponding Mie scattering output, while Fig. 10-b presents the associated extinction behavior.

Following the observations in Figs. 9 and 10, the extinction spectra significantly depended on the concentration of the CQDs in the composite. As stated earlier, the extinction spectrum is mainly dominated by the forward scattering for wavelengths above 400 nm, as TiO2 is a UV absorber. Accordingly, the higher the extinction, the more the forward scattering is boosted. Based on that, we adopt the machine learning algorithm, namely random-forest, to provide a broad scanning vision on the impact of varying the CQDs concentration, not only within the dataset limit up to 12 %, but also to predict the permeance reaching 15 % concentration, cf—Fig. 11 -a. The output from the ML model is then seeded to MEEP, based on the model in Fig. 4, to provide the extinction spectra for different CQDs concentrations, seeking an optimum point where the extinction is maximum. A three-dimensional dataset of 6354 points was used to link the wavelength, the CQDs concentration, the effective scattering radius, and the porosity. Most of the data, 70 %, were used for the model training, while the remaining portion was divided equally for testing and validation. An overall accuracy of 89.84 % was achieved by applying hyperparameter optimization. Fig. 11-b demonstrates the dependence of the scattering on the CQD concentration at the central wavelength of 550 nm, indicating maximum extinction performance at a CQD concentration of 6.9 %, with extinction reaching 5.3. The maximum extinction spectrum at 6.9 % CQD concentration is plotted in Fig. 11-c. Finally, the variation of the primary two driving parameters, the effective scattering particle radius, and the porosity, against the CQDs concentration is introduced in Fig. 10-d. The plotted data analyzes the tradeoff between increasing the effective radius, approaching a wavelength scale, and the alternative effect of decreasing the porosity of the medium, leading to a bucky medium rather than a porous medium. This is attributed to the reduced forward scattering observed with a slightly high CQD concentration.

As previously mentioned, the present study showcases an extinction forward scattering extinction value of 5.3 with a CQD concentration of 6.9 %. Upon reviewing existing literature, other investigations have explored scattering mechanisms within both bare TiO2 [1] and modified porous mediums, with the latter involving the use of diverse dopants [2,4], including silver. In Table 2 , we provide a summary of previously reported data from the literature alongside our findings. Consequently, the results demonstrate that the optimized concentration of CQDs yielded the most significant enhancement in forward scattering. However, there are certain limitations that should be acknowledged. Firstly, the study's focus on thin films fabricated on microscopic glass may limit the generalizability of the findings to other substrates commonly used in photovoltaic devices, typically FTO, and ITO coated glass. Furthermore, the study's reliance on a specific range of CQD concentrations up to 15 % may not fully capture potential behaviors at higher concentrations, warranting further investigation. Finally, the broader implications of the findings for practical photovoltaic device performance and scalability could be explored in future work to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the potential impact of the observed forward scattering mechanisms.

Incorporating a forward-scattering medium, such as the proposed porous structures discussed in this paper, as a front layer in perovskite solar cells and dye-sensitized solar cells is essential in enhancing their performance, especially for diffused and low light intensity applications [2–4,8]. Forward scattering can increase the power conversion efficiency in solar cells operating under diffused or low light intensity conditions by enhancing light trapping and absorption within the active layer of the solar cell. When incident light interacts with the textured or nanostructured surface of the solar cell, forward scattering redirects a portion of the incident light into angles closer to the normal direction, increasing the optical path length traveled by the photons within the active material. This extended path length increases the probability of light absorption and carrier generation, thereby improving the utilization of low-intensity or diffused light. As a result, forward scattering helps to mitigate the reduced photon flux associated with low light conditions, ultimately leading to enhanced efficiency in solar cell performance, particularly in environments with limited direct sunlight or under overcast skies. The forward-scattering medium can also mitigate light trapping and reflection losses, promoting better utilization of incident light and reducing optical losses within the devices. Consequently, the strategic integration of TiO2 and CQDs as a forward-scattering layer is pivotal in optimizing the light management and overall photovoltaic performance of perovskite solar cells and DSSCs.

The study provides valuable insights into the forward scattering mechanisms in TiO2 with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and their potential implications for photovoltaic applications. Through experimental characterization of thin films on microscopic glass and the utilization of advanced techniques such as UV–Vis-IR spectrometer, SEM measurements, Mie scattering modeling, convolutional neural network-based algorithms, and machine learning models, the research identifies the optimum CQDs concentration for maximum forward scattering. The study presents compelling findings regarding the influence of CQD concentration on extinction spectra and forward scattering in TiO2 composites. Experimental characterization, machine learning algorithms, and numerical simulations identified an optimum CQD concentration of 6.9 % for achieving maximum extinction performance, with a corresponding maximum extinction of 5.3. The research also highlighted the tradeoff between increasing the effective scattering particle radius and decreasing the porosity of the medium against CQDs concentration, providing valuable insights into the factors influencing forward scattering and offering guidance for optimizing photovoltaic performance. These findings contribute to a deeper understanding of the mechanisms at play in TiO2 with CQDs and have significant implications for enhancing photovoltaic applications.
Not applicable.
The data supporting this study's findings are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
No funding.
The Authors declare no conflict of interest.
The data supporting this study's findings are from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

All authors have contributed equally.

All authors confirm their participation in this paper.
All authors accept the publication rules applied by the journal.

Mona Samir: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zahraa Ismail: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ahmed M. Agour: Formal analysis, Data curation. Hassan Nageh: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Sameh O. Abdellatif: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the support and contribution of The Centre of Emerging Learning Technologies (CELT), FabLab, and The Nanotechnology Research Centre (NTRC) at the British University in Egypt. Special thanks to Ms. Mona Samir, research assistant at NTRC, for her support in preparing NiO suspension.
